name: Weights & Biases (wandb)
tagline: Track, visualize, and version your machine learning experiments
metaDescription: Weights & Biases (wandb) is an open-source Python library for logging ML experiments, running hyperparameter sweeps, and versioning models and datasets.
overview: Weights & Biases (wandb) is an open-source Python client library for tracking machine learning experiments. You add a few lines of code to your training script, and it records your hyperparameters, metrics, and other run details so you can review and compare them later in the W&B web app.

It's built for ML engineers and researchers who want a record of every run instead of scattered logs and spreadsheets. As an experiment tracking tool, it sits alongside your existing training code and integrates with common ML frameworks, so you can add it to projects without rewriting them.

Beyond plain metric logging, wandb supports hyperparameter sweeps and versioning of models and datasets. The library is free and open source; the backend runs on W&B's hosted cloud or on infrastructure you manage yourself.
features: Log metrics, hyperparameters, and run metadata from a Python script or notebook with wandb.init() and run.log()
Visualize and compare runs in the W&B web app, including how metrics change across training steps
Run hyperparameter sweeps to search over configurations
Version models and datasets as artifacts
Integrates with popular ML frameworks and libraries for quick setup in existing projects
Choose hosted multi-tenant cloud, dedicated cloud, or self-managed deployment on your own infrastructure

gettingStarted.intro: Install the library, create an API key, then log a run from your training code.

steps.heading: Install the wandb library
steps.body: Install wandb from PyPI with pip.

steps.heading: Sign up and create an API key
steps.body: Sign up for a W&B account and create an API key at wandb.ai/settings. Optionally run wandb login to store it on your machine; otherwise W&B prompts you the first time you use it.

steps.heading: Track a training experiment
steps.body: In your Python script or notebook, initialize a run with wandb.init(), pass your hyperparameters as config, and log metrics with run.log().
steps.code:
import wandb

project = "my-awesome-project"
config = {"epochs": 1337, "lr": 3e-4}

with wandb.init(project=project, config=config) as run:
    # Training code here
    run.log({"accuracy": 0.9, "loss": 0.1})

useCases: Keep a searchable record of every training run's hyperparameters and metrics instead of manual logs or spreadsheets
Compare accuracy and loss curves across many runs to pick the best configuration
Run hyperparameter sweeps to find good settings for a model
Version datasets and trained models so results stay reproducible over time
